Vacancy hopping is widely regarded as a predominant dif-
fusion mechanism in crystalline, as vacancies provide a low-
energy pathway for atomic migration.  In ab initio calcula-
tions,  two representative methods are commonly used to in-

vestigate hopping mechanisms: the nudged elastic band (NEB)
method and a method based on the Einstein relation. The NEB
method identifies an optimum hopping path and yields the hop-
ping barrier (Ea), the energy gain at the transition state (TS).
This method is inherently path-dependent and does not pro-
vide any insight into the correlation between hopping paths,
making it unsuitable for assessing overall diffusion.  Mean-
while,  the Einstein relation approach statistically determines
the diffusion coefficient (D) from ab initio molecular dynam-
ics (AIMD) simulations.[10] However, since vacancies are not
directly observable in AIMD simulations, this approach applies
only to atoms and not to vacancies; therefore, it is not suitable
for studying vacancy hopping. Moreover, it does not provide
some key hopping parameters such as hopping distance (a),
jump attempt frequency (ν), and correlation factor ( f ), which
are essential for accurately modeling ionic drift and diffusion
via vacancy hopping.[8, 11, 12, 13, 14] The key parameters for
vacancy hopping are listed in Table 1, which can be used to
express all the other quantities required for hopping,  includ-
ing D.[15] Still, due to the lack of an established method for
determining their effective values, many device models have
either assumed arbitrary values for these parameters or over-
looked their impact.[8, 16, 17]
In  this work, we  introduce VacHopPy  an open-source
Python package for analyzing vacancy hopping mechanisms
April 1, 2025

2.1. Vacancy trajectory determination
In AIMD simulations, vacancies are not directly observable.
Instead, they are identified as unoccupied lattice sites after all
atoms are assigned to their respective sites, which are deter-
mined based on the TS distribution rather than proximity alone.
At temperature T = 0 K, the TS distribution can be inferred
from the force vector (F) acting on an atom.  As shown in
the potential energy surface (PES, represented in gray shade)
in Fig.  1(a), an atom that has not passed the TS (yellow)
experiences the F directed toward the initial site, satisfying
cos θi > cos θ f , where θi and θ f  represent the angles between F
and the directions toward the initial and final sites, respectively.
In contrast, the F acting on an atom that has crossed the TS
(blue) is directed toward the final site, satisfying cos θi < cos θ f .
At T > 0 K, as shown in Fig. 1(b), however, thermal fluctua-
tions generate random perturbations that complicate the deter-
mination of the TS distribution from instantaneous F.  Since
these perturbations are random,  they can be effectively miti-
gated by time averaging. Thus, by employing the PES averaged
in time, the TS distribution can be estimated based on F; hence,
the lattice occupancy can be accurately assigned.

Figure 1:  (a) PES diagram at T = 0 K. F acting on the atom before passing
the TS (yellow) is directed toward the initial site (cos θi > cos θ f ), while that
after passing the TS (blue) is directed toward the final site (cos θi < cos θ f ). θi
and θ f denote the angles between the F and the directions toward the initial and
final sites, respectively. (b) PES diagram at T > 0 K. The PES is perturbed by
random thermal fluctuations, making it challenging to determine the TS based
only on F
Fig. 2 summarizes the algorithm implemented in VacHopPy
for vacancy trajectory determination. All notations in this study
are listed in Table S1 of supplementary material. The process
begins by averaging the coordinate (ρ) and F of each atom from
AIMD data over a user-defined time interval (tinterval). Each av-
eraged dataset corresponds to a single step, with the total num-
ber of steps determined by dividing the simulation time (t) by
the tinterval  (t/tinterval). The optimization of tinterval will be de-
scribed in section 4. Subsequently, at each step, atoms are ini-
tially assigned to their nearest lattice sites to construct prelimi-
nary configurations. To refine these proximity-based occupan-
cies using TS criteria, VacHopPy iteratively tracks changes in
lattice site occupancy and evaluates their validity using the cor-
responding F whenever atomic movement is detected. A move-
ment is considered valid if cos θi < cos θ f ;  in this case,  the
lattice occupancy is updated accordingly. Otherwise, the move-
ment is disregarded. By iterating this process from the initial
step onward, the evolution of lattice occupancy by atoms and
vacancies is systematically determined. As a result, VacHopPy
provides trajectories projected onto the lattice sites, eliminating
thermal fluctuations and allowing users to clearly track the hop-
ping process. Potential issues such as multi-hopping and multi-
vacancy, which may arise during this process, are summarized
in Supplementary note 1 of supplementary material and are au-
tomatically resolved in the code.
After determining the trajectory, VacHopPy categorizes va-
cancy hopping events based on Voronoi  tessellation-based
neighbor  analyses.[18] Non-vacancy-hopping mechanisms,
such as the kick-out mechanism, are identified and labeled as
unknown (e.g., unknown1, unknown2). Finally, VacHopPy pro-
vides a summary of the vacancy hopping history,  including

2.2. Effective lattice approximation
Extracting a single set of effective hopping parameters is
equivalent to converting an original lattice having multiple va-
cancy sites and multiple hopping paths into an effective lattice
characterized by a single vacancy site and a single hopping path
while maintaining the overall diffusion characteristics of the
original lattice. The single set of effective hopping parameters
is obtained by integrating all the hopping paths in the original
lattice, properly considering their energetic, kinetic, and geo-
metric characteristics.  This approach, named effective lattice
approximation, is schematized in Fig. 3.

To formulate the effec-
tive hopping parameters, an arbitrary lattice comprising n sym-
metrically non-equivalent vacancy sites is considered, where

path is represented as x(i, j). Furthermore, any effective hopping
parameter is denoted with a bar (x¯).
From the vacancy hopping history, the total number of hop-
ping events (c), the number of hopping events via the (i, j) path

All ab initio calculations were conducted using the Vienna
Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP)[25, 26] with the Perdew-
Burke-Ernzerhof  (PBE)[27] exchange-correlation functional.
For the fcc Al system, a single neutral vacancy (VAl) was intro-
duced, while for rutile TiO2 and mono HfO2 systems, a doubly
2+
charged oxygen vacancy (VO ) was considered.
AIMD simulations were conducted in canonical (NVT) en-
sembles using the Nose-Hoover thermoset.[28] To accelerate
the diffusion, simulations were conducted near the melting tem-
perature (Tmelt) or the phase transition temperature.  For Al
(Tmelt = 933 K), simulations were performed at 750-900 K.
For rutile TiO2 (Tmelt = 2116 K), simulations were conducted
at 1700-2100K. For mono HfO2 (Tmelt = 3031 K), simulations

The latest version of the VacHopPy package, including ex-
ample files and user instructions,  is available on the GitHub
repository (https://github.com/TY-Jeong/VacHopPy) and can
be easily installed via pip (e.g., pip install vachoppy). To
simplify usage, VacHopPy supports a command-line interface
(CLI), with its workflow and available CLI commands summa-
rized in Fig. 4.
The vachoppy -m t command reads VASP-style AIMD
data and generates an animation (traj.gif) visualizing the va-
cancy trajectory, offering a clear representation of the diffu-
sion process. The core functionality of VacHopPy is executed
through the vachoppy -m p command, which outputs both the
vacancy hopping history and the effective hopping parameters
(except for z¯ and ν¯), with the results stored in parameter.txt.
This command requires tinterval, which can be determined via
5

      
            
                       
                                   
             
             
                                           
                    
                               
                             
                                  
                                             
mmands (vachoppy -m t, vachoppy -m p, vachoppy -m pp, and vachoppy
.  For the time-consuming commands, vachoppy -m p and vachoppy -m f,
modules and classes (or methods) in the VacHopPy architecture are shown.
convergence tests (a typical value is around 0.1 ps, though it
depends on the system; see Supplementary note 2 of supple-
mentary material). Following this, the vachoppy -m pp com-
mand can be executed to obtain z¯, ν¯, and ν(i, j). This command
reads two input files, parameter.txt and neb.csv, with the lat-
ter containing Ea values for all the vacancy hopping paths in
the original lattice obtained by NEB calculations. Therefore,
the determination of the single set of effective hopping param-
eters becomes a two-step process: first, calculating E¯a, a¯, and
f  from the AIMD data, and second, calculating z¯ and ν¯ using
the NEB data. Lastly, the vachoppy -m f command compares
the AIMD snapshots and the reference phase and outputs their
structural similarity as a function of time.  The similarity is
quantified by dcos(x), the cosine distance from a reference phase
x, where lower dcos(x) indicates higher similarity (see Appendix
C for a detailed description). Furthermore, two time-consuming
commands, vachoppy -m p and vachoppy -m f, can be par-
allelized using mpirun to improve performance on large sys-
tems.
To ensure reliable analysis, sufficient sampling of hopping
events is required. To overcome the limited sampling of AIMD
simulations due to short simulation times, VacHopPy processes
multiple AIMD datasets simultaneously. For example, using 20
AIMD datasets, each with a simulation time of 1 ns, effectively
corresponds to a 20 ns simulation. A detailed description of the
AIMD data is provided in Fig.S1 of supplementary material.

5.1. Fcc Al
Fig. 5 shows the atomic structure of fcc Al, which features
a single site and a single hopping path for VAl  named the A1
path (n = 1 and m1 = 1). The accompanying table summarizes
the properties of this path. The Ea of 0.52 eV, obtained using
NEB calculations, agrees well with previous theoretical stud-
ies (0.52-0.63 eV)[33] and an experimental observation (0.59
eV)[34].

Figure 5: Atomic structure and a single vacancy hopping path in fcc Al. Light
and dark grey circles represent Al atoms in different layers. The properties of
the A1 path are summarized in the accompanying table.
The effective hopping parameters for fcc Al were obtained
by executing the commands vachoppy -m p Al 0.1 and
vachoppy -m pp in sequence, where the two arguments in
the first command specify the vacancy type and tinterval, respec-
tively. The results are summarized in Table 2. For T-dependent
parameters z¯, ν¯, and f ,  the mean values over the simulated
T range are presented, with standard deviations indicated in
parentheses.  Their T-dependencies are shown in Fig.  S2 of
supplementary material, where they are found to be negligible.
Since this system contains only a single hopping path,  the
effective lattice remains identical to the original lattice. As a
result, the effective parameters, a¯, E¯a, and z¯, were calculated to
be almost identical to their original values, as shown in Table 2.
Additionally,  f  is in good agreement with the reference value
obtained by Monte Carlo simulations.[36] The ab initio-based
f calculation is an advantage unique to VacHopPy compared to
other existing codes.[10, 37] The ν, on the other hand, is highly
method-dependent. Our calculation based on Erying’s formula
yields νA1 = 3.0 THz (see Supplementary note 3 of supple-
mentary material), while a previous study reported νA1 of 4.9
THz and 7.0 THz using density functional perturbation theory
(DFPT) and finite difference method (FDM), respectively.[35]

Averaged ν over the simulated T range for T1 and T2 paths, while that for the T3
the T1, T2, and T3 paths, respectively. The negligible contri-
bution of the T3 path is attributed to its significantly high Ea,
whereas the dominant contribution of the T2 path is due to its
zT2 value being eight times higher than that of T1, despite the
higher Ea.
Figs.  7(a)-(c) show f , z¯, and ν¯ as functions of T, with the
mean values represented by dotted lines. While f shows neg-
ligible T-dependence, z¯ increases, and ν¯ decreases as T rises.
This behavior is attributed to the increased contribution of the
T2 path at higher T. At low T, hopping primarily occurs via
the T1 path, which has the lowest Ea. As T increases, however,
thermal energies promote hopping via the T2 path. Therefore,
the high zT2 leads to an overall increase in z¯ with T. In contrast,
smaller νT2 than νT1 in Fig. 7(d) results in a decrease in ν¯ with
T. Note that νT3 is omitted from Fig. 7(d) due to its low relia-
bility; since the hopping events via the T3 path are not sufficient
for obtaining a reliable νT3 by VacHopPy which determines ν
statistically.
The higher νT1 suggests that thermal vibrations are biased
toward the T1 path. Such anisotropic thermal vibration should
be considered to accurately determine the contribution of each
path, which has been frequently overlooked in previous device
simulations. Instead, they assumed a typical ν value in the range
of 1-10 THz, disregarding its path-dependence, presumably be-
cause conventional TST calculations occasionally yield unreli-
able ν values.[40, 41] For instance, νT2 calculated using Ery-
9
ing’s formula was found to be about 10 THz, far exceeding
the typical range of 1-10 THz. (see Fig. N6(d) in Supplemen-
tary note 3) This discrepancy is probably due to the presence
of abundant imaginary phonon modes. Since TST-based Ery-
ing’s formula highly relies on phonon spectra, their applicabil-

ity is limited to systems containing only stable phonon modes.
However, for systems containing vacancies, imaginary phonons
commonly appear, thereby undermining the reliability of such
calculations.  In contrast, the statistical approach implemented
in VacHopPy provides a more robust determination of ν, as long
as sufficient hopping events are sampled.

The calculated E¯a and
ν¯ show good agreement with experimental data, 1.1 eV and 5
THz[42] and 1.15 eV and 0.3-6.3 THz[43].
In summary, VacHopPy accurately determines the contribu-
tion of each hopping path in rutile TiO2 by incorporating en-
ergetic, kinetic, and geometric factors, along with anisotropic
thermal vibrations.  Consequently,  it provides a physically
meaningful single set of effective hopping parameters that re-
flects the characteristics of rutile TiO2.

5.3.1. Low-T region (T ≤ 2000 K)
The effective hopping parameters for mono HfO2 were ob-
tained by executing the commands vachoppy -m p O 0.05
followed by vachoppy -m pp. The shorter tinterval compared
to the two previous example systems, fcc Al and rutile TiO2,
reflects a fast hopping process in mono HfO2 (e.g.,  the F1
path has a significantly low Ea value of 0.12 eV). From the
AIMD data, a total of 4,443 hopping events (excluding 33 non-
vacancy-hopping events) were detected. Among them, 3,346
(75%) events correspond to H-paths, and 1,097 (25%) events
correspond to F-paths. Furthermore, vacancies spend about 24
times longer at the H-sites than at the F-sites (tH ≈ 24tF).
Fig. 9(a) shows the ν values for paths with low Ea (H1, H2,
and H3 for the H-site; F1 and F2 for the F-site) averaged over
the simulated T range. For comparison, the calculations were
conducted using two methods: VacHopPy (solid bars) and Ery-
ing’s formula in TST (x-patterned bars, see Supplementary note
3). Both methods show similar trends, with the H3 path ex-
hibiting a significantly higher ν relative to the others, suggest-
ing the thermal vibration at the H-site is highly biased toward
the H3 path. The slight discrepancies of ν values between the
two methods might be originated from the harmonic approxi-
mation used in phonon calculations or the presence of imagi-
nary phonon modes in mono HfO2 (see Fig. N7 of supplemen-
tary material).

all movements following the H3 path. These findings suggest
that the H3 path can play an important role in the diffusion pro-

The effective hopping parameters for mono HfO are sum-
2
marized in Table 4. As shown in Figs. 9(b) and (c), five ma-
jor paths, H1, H2, H3, F1, and F2, dominate the overall diffu-
sion process. Considering their contributions, the obtained and
a¯ and E¯a appear reasonable, as they are similar to or slightly
lower than the corresponding value for the H3 path, the most
dominant one. Notably,  the calculated E¯a of 0.70 eV aligns
well with experimental measurements (0.67-0.72 eV[47], 0.48
eV[48] and molecular dynamics (MD) results (0.66 eV[49]).
The experimentally measured or MD-obtained Ea values corre-
spond to E¯a, as all hopping paths are inherently considered.
Fig.  9(d) shows z¯ as a function of T, with its T-averaged
value indicated by the black dotted line. While z¯ remains nearly
constant up to 1900 K, a notable increase in z¯ is observed at
2000 K. To analyze the origin of this increase, two parameters,

⟨z⟩, and ⟨m⟩, are additionally introduced as red circles and blue
triangles, respectively, with their mean values indicated by dot-
ted lines. The ⟨z⟩ represents the effective z per path type (i.e.,
mean z(i, j)), and ⟨m⟩ indicates the effective number of path types
(i.e., mean m(i, j)) participating in the hopping process, which
are expressed in Appendix D. By definition, z¯ = ⟨z⟩ · ⟨m⟩ holds.
While ⟨z⟩ remains nearly constant with T, an abrupt increase in
⟨m⟩ is observed at 2000 K, clearly demonstrating that this is the
primary factor in the increase in z¯ at this T. This finding is con-
sistent with the contributions of various path types identified in
Figs. 9 (b) and (c). Meanwhile, Fig. 9(e) shows ν¯ as a func-
tion of T, with its mean value indicated by a dotted line. The ν¯
follows an inverse trend to z¯ since the relation ν¯ = 1/z¯τ¯0 holds,
where the factor τ¯0 is treated as a constant in our framework
(see Eq. (11)).
Fig. 9(f) shows f as a function of T, with the inset display-
ing an Arrhenius fit. Unlike the negligible T-dependencies of
f observed in fcc Al and rutile TiO2, a notable increase in f
occurs at T ≥ 1800 K. This trend may be attributed to the un-
stable mono lattice near the onset of the phase transition to the
tet phase, which is known to occur around 2000 K. Near this T
range, the mono and the tet lattices might coexist, enhancing the
randomness in atomic motions, thereby a rise in f . The activa-
f
tion energy for f (Ea ) is determined to be 0.22 eV, a factor that
should be considered to accurately describe the T-dependence
of D at this T range (see Eq. (12)).
Compared to fcc Al and rutile TiO2, the f in mono HfO2 is
2+
relatively low, implying that diffusion of V  in this system is
O
highly correlated. One major cause for the low f  is the rela-
tion between the H1 and F1 paths.

The H1 path represents the
hopping from the H-site to the F-site, while the F1 path is the
exact opposite. Considering the significantly low Ea for the F1
path and the higher energetic state of the F-site by 0.56 eV, a
2+
VO  that has moved via the H1 path tends to return via the F1
path, indicating a strong correlation between these two paths.
Fig.  9(g) shows the return probabilities, the likelihood that a
2+
VO  returns to its original site after performing a hop, for the
major paths obtained using VacHopPy. As expected, the H1
path exhibits a return probability of 76%, more than twice that
of other paths. To sum up, VacHopPy computes the effective
hopping parameters in mono HfO2 by incorporating factors that
are often neglected in other frameworks. First, it accounts for
the anisotropic thermal vibration effects that influence the con-
tribution of each hopping path. Second, it considers the impact
of an unstable lattice or thermal fluctuations on f . This com-
prehensive approach leads to effective hopping parameters that
more accurately reflect the complex dynamics of the system.
5.3.2. High-T region (T > 2000 K)
At T > 2000 K, the mono HfO2 becomes unstable and under-
goes a phase transition to the tet HfO ˙In such unstable lattices,
2
the center of atomic vibration may shift away from the original
lattice point, resulting in poorly defined vacancy. Consequently,
the vacancy trajectory determination, as well as the effective
hopping parameter calculation, may lack accuracy. The stabil-
ity of the mono HfO2 was evaluated using the vachoppy -m
f 0.05 20 0.04 0.04 command, where the arguments cor-
1

respond to tinterval, Rmax, ∆, and σ in sequence (see Appendix C
and the GitHub repository for descriptions for the arguments).
Fig.  10(a) shows the variation in dcos(mono) during the
AIMD simulations conducted at three low-T (1600 K, 1800 K,
and 2000 K) and three high-T (2200 K, 2400 K, and 2600 K)
ranges. For comparison, the traces of dcos(mono) at each T are
arranged vertically; hence, the absolute y-values are meaning-
less. Instead, the focus is on the relative changes in dcos(mono)
over time. In the low-T range, dcos(mono) remains nearly con-
stant, indicating a stable mono lattice. In contrast, in the high-T
range, dcos(mono) exhibits substantial fluctuations, suggesting
lattice instability and the onset of a phase transition.  It is im-
portant to note that the lattice parameters were constrained to
those of the mono phase since the AIMD simulations were per-
formed in the NVT ensemble. As a result, any deformed lattice
is not maintained but tends to revert to the mono lattice, leaving
a peak in the dcos(mono) trace.
Fig.  10(b) shows a magnified view of the peak,  indicated
by the dashed box in Fig.  10(a).  For comparison, three rep-
resentative phases are also shown as reference phases: antipo-
lar orthorhombic (ao, Pbca), polar orthorhombic (po, Pca21),
and tet. The atomic structures of these phases are provided in
Fig. S5 of supplementary material. Notably, as dcos(mono) in-
creases, dcos(tet) decreases, while dcos(ao) and dcos(po) remain
nearly constant.  This finding clearly suggests that the phase
transition is directed toward the tet phase.  To verify this, a
structural snapshot at the peak, indicated by an arrow in Fig.
10(b), was freely relaxed, and the evolution is shown in Fig.
10(c). The left panel of Fig. 10(c) represents the initial struc-
ture of the snapshot, which is the 2×2×2 supercell of mono lat-
2+
tice containing one V  (a = 10.27 Å, b = 10.38 Å, c = 10.64
O
Å, α = 90.0°, β = 99.7°, and γ = 90.0°). Unlike the well-
separated 3-coordinated oxygen atoms in mono HfO2 in Fig.
8, the oxygen atoms in this snapshot cluster together, resem-
bling the 4-coordinated oxygen configuration of the tet phase.
The lowest optical phonon mode may contribute to this clus-
tering, as two neighboring 3-coordinated oxygen atoms move
toward each other in this vibrational mode, as indicated by a
dotted blue ellipse in Fig.  S3(b).  The middle panel of Fig.
10(c) shows an intermediate stage during relaxation, where the
atomic structure closely resembles the tet phase. (In this struc-
ture, red and orange circles all the mean 4-coordinated sites.)
The cell size of this structure is a = 10.12 Å, b = 10.11 Å,
c = 10.32 Å, α = 90.9°, β = 89.8°, and γ = 90.7°, which are
comparable to that of the tet 2×2×2 supercell (a = b = 10.15 Å,
c = 10.45 Å, α = β = γ = 90.0°). The minor deviation is likely
2+
due to the presence of VO . However,  the relaxation contin-
ues until the structure in the right panel of Fig. 10(c) emerges,
featuring a wavelike arrangement of oxygens, indicative of an
antipolar characteristic. However,  the overall lattice remains
mono (a = 10.22 Å, b = 10.22 Å, c = 10.25 Å, α = 90.3°,
2+
β = 89.8°, and γ = 95.4°, minor deviations due to V  ). The
O
atomic arrangement in the final structure resembles the antipo-
lar Pnma phase, which is depicted in a pseudo-cubic represen-
tation in Fig.

S5(e) (although the Pnma phase is orthorhombic,
it appears mono in this representation).[50] This final structure
0.

In this work,  the advantages of VacHopPy were demon-
strated using three example systems. First, VacHopPy enables
the automated detection and characterization of atomic or va-
cancy hopping events, providing insights into the overall diffu-
sion process at the atomic scale. For instance, the collective dif-
fusion observed in mono HfO2 reveals a preference for oxygen
atoms to diffuse along the c-axis (or the H3 path). Second, Va-
cHopPy provides physically reasonable effective hopping pa-
rameters by incorporating geometric, energetic, and anisotropic
thermal vibrations effects. Specifically, in the mono HfO2, the
anisotropy in ν, a factor often overlooked in previous simula-
tions but inherently considered in this framework, plays an im-
portant role in determining the effective parameters. Further-
more, in this framework, the T-dependent diffusion behaviors,
represented by D and τ, are simplified into a simple Arrhenius
equation composed of the effective parameters, with its valid-
ity confirmed over a wide T range spanning several hundred

Kelvins. These properties makes the effective hopping param-
eters suitable for device-scale simulations. Third, VacHopPy
offers a tool for monitoring phase transitions during AIMD sim-
ulations. The phase transition from the mono to the tet phase in
HfO2 was successfully captured at 2200 K. We expect that Va-
cHopPy will play a crucial role in multiscale modeling by pro-
viding a robust framework for bridging atomistic and device-
scale simulations.

expression proposed by Oganov et al. is employed.[54] Finger-
print vector accounting for a pair of atom types A and B (ψAB)
is given by:

Here, ρ(i, j)  is the interatomic distance between atom Ai and

a threshold distance (Rmax) from the Ai atom. NA and NB are
the numbers of atoms of each type, respectively. V and ∆ are
the unit cell volume and the bin size, respectively. The function
δ is a Gaussian-smeared delta function characterized by stan-
dard deviation (σ), introduced to improve the convergence of
the calculation.  In VacHopPy,  the fingerprint of a system is
generated by concatenating ψAB for all possible combinations
of A and B, serving as a robust fingerprint of the structure due
to its independencies on transition, rotation, and mirror opera-
tions. Using Eq. (C.1), ψAB can be generated for each snapshot
in an AIMD simulation. Changes in atomic configurations are
quantified using the cosine distance (dcos), defined as:
